coredata.dc:title: Transformation of bulk MnO2 to fluorescent quantum dots for selective and sensitive detection of ferric ions and ascorbic acid by turn-off-on strategy

Maity, Banibrata
Basu, Soumen

Sensor
Dynamic quenching
Real sample detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of citric acid-MnO2 quantum dots (CA-MnO2 QDs)
Procedure for detection of Fe3+ ions and AA
Sensing of Fe3+ ions by CA-MnO2 QDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Neeraj sohal is grateful to CSIR (Grant no: 01 (2955)/18/EMR-II) for fellowship. Dr. Banibrata Maity is also thankful to Seed Grant Project, TIET, Patiala for research funding. Also, the authors are thankful for the TEM facility, funded by a TPF Nanomission, GOI project at the Centre for Nano and Soft Matter Sciences, Bengaluru, FESEM facility to TIET, Patiala. The authors are grateful to Dr. Nimai Mishra, SRM University, Amaravati, for lifetime analysis. The authors are also thankful to Reviewers for their careful revision and valuable comments which improved our manuscript quality.

Neeraj sohal is grateful to CSIR (Grant no: 01 (2955)/18/EMR-II) for fellowship. Dr. Banibrata Maity is also thankful to Seed Grant Project, TIET, Patiala for research funding. Also, the authors are thankful for the TEM facility, funded by a TPF Nanomission, GOI project at the Centre for Nano and Soft Matter Sciences, Bengaluru, FESEM facility to TIET, Patiala. The authors are grateful to Dr. Nimai Mishra, SRM University, Amaravati, for lifetime analysis. The authors are also thankful to Reviewers for their careful revision and valuable comments which improved our manuscript quality.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) and (b) shows the FESEM images of bulk MnO2, and (c) EDS spectra of bulk MnO2.
(a) and (b) represents the TEM images of CA-MnO2 QDs, (c) particle size distribution, (d) SAED pattern, and (e) EDS spectrum of CA-MnO2 QDs.
(a) XRD pattern, (b) XPS survey spectrum, (c) XPS graph of Mn2p and (d) O1s of CA-MnO2 QDs.

(a) Bar graph representing the PL intensity of MnO2 QDs in the presence of different biomolecules, (b) UV–visible absorbance, excitation, and emission spectra of CA-MnO2 QDs, (c) PL emission spectra of CA-MnO2 QDs at different excitation wavelengths, and (d) bar graph showing the PL intensity of CA-MnO2 QDs at different irradiation time.

(a) Relative percentage change in PL intensity of CA-MnO2 QDs in the presence of different metal ions, and (b) Selectivity image of prepared system with different metal ions under the UV light of 365 nm wavelength in which blank corresponds to CA-MnO2 QDs only.

(a) The interference study of CA-MnO2 QDs for Fe3+ in the presence of different metal ions, (b) variation of PL intensity of CA-MnO2 QDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions, (c) Stern-Volmer plot between F0/F and different concentrations of Fe3+ ions, and (d) a linear relationship between PL responses (F0-F/F0) and various concentrations of Fe3+ ions (0–10 µM) with inset image of a linear relationship between PL responses (F0-F/F0) and various concentrations of Fe3+ ions (0–0.6 µM).

(a) The PL intensity of CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ sensing system in the presence of different analytes, and (b) Selectivity image of prepared system with different metal ions under the UV light of 365 nm wavelength in which blank corresponds to CA-MnO2 QDs only.

(a) The interference study of CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ sensing system for AA with different analytes, (b) The variation in PL intensity of CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ with the addition of AA, (c) A linear relationship of PL responses (F-F0/F0) vs concentration of AA (0–25 µM) and inset image of shows a linear plot of PL responses (F-F0/F0) and different concentrations of AA (0–1.0 µM), and (d) Fluorescence lifetime decays of CA-MnO2 QDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions and AA.
Schematic illustration of synthesis of citric acid functionalized MnO2 QDs.
The different sensing platforms for the detection of Fe3+ ions with LODs values.

head.ce:title: Transformation of bulk MnO2 to fluorescent quantum dots for selective and sensitive detection of ferric ions and ascorbic acid by turn-off-on strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Thapar Institute of Engineering and Technology Patiala 147004, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Thapar Institute of Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Thapar Institute of Engineering and Technology Patiala 147004, India

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Among various inorganic materials, manganese oxide (MnO2) has gained large attention in different research fields such as electrocatalysis, supercapacitors, drug delivery, photothermal treatment, and optical sensing due to exceptional large charge transfer rate, high active surface area, non-toxicity, and cost-effectiveness [1,2]. MnO2 has been largely used as a dynamic material for the optical sensing of biomolecules (such as glutathione, ascorbic acid, cysteine, etc.) due to its exceptional physical, electrochemical, and optical properties [3,4]. The exclusive light absorption capability and redox features of MnO2 nanostructure lead to consider it as fluorescence quenchers and further, used in the preparation of “turn off–on” fluorescence sensing probes [5,6]. We have already reported “turn off–on” biosensors for the detection of glutathione, based on carbon dots-MnO2 nanostructure in which carbon dots act as a fluorescent probe and MnO2 nanostructure act as a recognizer [3,7]. The combination of fluorescent probes and MnO2 nanostructure as sensing platforms with synergistic properties are reported in the literature for the detection of different biomolecules. These biosensors need a fluorescent probe such as carbon dots (Cdots) [7], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [8], and dyes [9] which are highly responsible for their sensing activity. Moreover, various reductive biomolecules can be oxidized by MnO2 nanostructure due to its oxidizing property that will confine the selectivity of sensing platforms [10]. Hence, the synthesis of a self-fluorescent MnO2 nanomaterial with particular functional groups is highly needed.

Kumar et al.[11] synthesized fluorescent few layered MnO2 nanosheets (FL-MnO2 NSs) through hydrothermal method in presence of N, N-dimethyl formamide and further, its surface is modified with amine-rich polyethyleneimine (PEI) to improve quantum yield. Furthermore, they used the prepared sensor for the detection of 2, 4, 6-trinitrotoulene (TNT). Recently, self-fluorescent MnO2 quantum dots (MnO2 QDs) were first time developed by Ma and co-workers [10] by ultrasonication method. They found that MnO2 QDs have good biocompatibility and a less toxic nature which makes them a good alternative for other QDs [10]. The fluorescence intensity and photostability of MnO2 QDs was improved by addition of bovine serum albumin (BSA) or cysteine (Cys). The cysteine-stabilized MnO2 QDs was used as a selective sensing system for dopamine detection based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism [12]. Mostly, Cdots were used as fluorescent probe in the presence of MnO2 nanosheets for glutathione sensing. Recently, Li et. al.[13] used Cys-MnO2 nanospheres with MnO2 nanosheet as “switch-on” sensor for glutathione detection. The fluorescence quantum yield shown by above MnO2 QDs was found to be less. So, there is need to improve fluorescence quantum yield and it can be achieved by synthesis of MnO2 QDs in the presence of other stabilizing agents. These stabilized MnO2 QDs can be used for sensing application.

Fe3+ ions play an essential part in various biological processes in living organisms, such as oxygen storage, development of red cells, enzyme catalysis, oxygen transportation, etc. [14]. The irregular range of Fe3+ ions in the human body can become a reason for several diseases, for example, anaemia, malaria, cancer, Parkinson’s disease, and Alzheimer’s disease [15]. World Health Organization (WHO) has reported that the amount of Fe must be less than 0.3 mg L-1 in surface or ground water, otherwise it become toxic metal [16]. Thus, the sensitive determination of Fe3+ ions has been seeking great attention for the safety of human health. Similarly, ascorbic acid (AA, also called vitamin C) acts as an antioxidant and also plays a significant role in different biochemical processes in living organisms and its quantitative detection is very helpful in pharmaceutical analysis [17,18]. Mostly, Cdots [19], GQDs, metal organic framework [20], and other QDs were used as sensor for the detection of Fe3+ and AA. In contrast, AA was also detected by MnO2 based sensors consist of any fluorescent probe [21]. There was no work reported for the detection of Fe3+ and AA by using self-fluorescent MnO2 QDs.

So, we have synthesized MnO2 QDs functionalized with citric acid (CA-MnO2 QDs) from bulk MnO2. The obtained CA-MnO2 QDs showed high photoluminescence (PL) intensity, good water solubility, and excellent photostability. Citric acid act as an acidifying agent which helps in the exfoliation and breaking of bulk MnO2 to MnO2 QDs. Therefore, the presence of citric acid is responsible for the exclusive optical properties of the prepared CA-MnO2 QDs. The PL intensity of CA-MnO2 QDs was highly quenched in the presence of Fe3+ ions based on a dynamic quenching mechanism which was confirmed by determining the various photophysical parameters. Further, the PL intensity of the CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ system was recovered with the addition of AA based on the redox reaction.

Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) (99 %), sodium nitrite (NaNO2) (99 %), and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) (98 %) were obtained from Loba Chemie, India, for the preparation of bulk MnO2. For the functionalization of MnO2 QDs, citric acid (99 %), glutathione (99 %), ascorbic acid (99 %), and l-cysteine (99 %) were used and purchased from Loba Chemie, India. Ferric nitrate (Fe(NO3)3) with 99 % purity was also obtained from Loba Chemie, India. All the stock solutions were formed in the ultrapure deionized (DI) water.

Bulk MnO2 was synthesized by using KMnO4 (5 mM) and NaNO2 (7.5 mM) as precursors via mixing both in 40 ml of water. Then, the gradual addition of 2 ml of H2SO4 (0.5 M) in the above solution along with constant stirring was done [22]. The color of the solution changes to brown which confirms the formation of MnO2. Further, the solution was centrifuged, and the obtained brown precipitates were washed with water and ethanol. The final obtained product was dried in an oven at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of citric acid-MnO2 quantum dots (CA-MnO2 QDs)
ce:section.ce:para: CA-MnO2 QDs were prepared by using bulk MnO2 as a precursor by using the ultra-sonication method. In detail, 10 mg of bulk MnO2 and 10 mg of citric acid were mixed in 10 ml of distilled water. Further, the above solution was sonicated for 10 h and then centrifuged for a continuous 30 min. at 10,000 rpm and the supernatant was collected as CA-MnO2 QDs (Scheme 1 ).

The UV–visible absorption spectrum of prepared CA-MnO2 QDs was obtained by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2600). The PL emission spectra were recorded via a spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, RF-6000). The morphology and size of bulk MnO2 were obtained by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). The morphology, shape, and size of CA-MnO2 QDs were obtained by transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis (JEOL, JEM 2100 plus). The crystal structure of the prepared CA-MnO2 QDs was examined via an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, PANALYTICAL X’Pert PRO) in which Cu Kα (λ = 1.540˚A) act as a source of radiation (0◦≤ 2θ ≤ 90◦). Time resolved fluorescence studies were performed by time-correlated single photon counting by TCSPC spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon IBH) with excitation laser source at 375 nm. Particle size distribution was observed by using dynamic light scattering method (DLS, Zetasizer, Malvern). Mass plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (MPAES) was used for validation of real sample analysis for Fe3+ ions. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, HPLC instrument) was used to validate the real sample analysis for AA.

The fluorescence quantum yield of prepared CA-MnO2 QDs was calculated by using quinine sulfate [23] as a reference (ΦR = 0.546) on the basis of equation (1):
(1)
ϕ S
=
ϕ R
×
A S
A R
×
( A b s )
R
( A b s )
S
×
η
S
2
η
R
2

The subscripts “S” and “R” are signified as the sample solution (prepared CA-MnO2 QDs) and reference (quinine sulfate), respectively. “ΦS ” and “ΦR ” denote the fluorescence quantum yield of the prepared CA-MnO2 QDs, and reference, respectively. “Abs” indicates absorbance, “A” indicates PL emission area, and “η” indicates refractive index.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for detection of Fe3+ ions and AA
ce:section.ce:para: The specific amount (1 ml) of prepared CA-MnO2 QDs (1 mg/ml) was added in the cuvette and diluted with 1 ml of distilled water. The PL emission spectra of CA-MnO2 QDs was recorded at 300 nm excitation wavelength. Further, the different concentrations of Fe3+ ions from 0.1 µM to 10 µM was added to the above 2 ml solution and the PL emission spectra of CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ was observed. Further, the specific amount (10 µM) of Fe3+ ions was added to CA-MnO2 QDs and then, the different concentrations (0.2 µM to 25 µM) of AA was added. After, the few minutes (5–6 min) of incubation time, the PL emission spectra of CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+-AA was observed.

CA-MnO2 QDs were developed by using bulk MnO2 as a precursor and citric acid as an exfoliating agent through the ultra-sonication method. The preparation method of CA-MnO2 QDs includes the exfoliation of bulk MnO2 into QDs in the presence of citric acid, which acts as an acidifying agent and stabilizing agent [24]. The morphology and shape of prepared bulk MnO2 were investigated by FESEM analysis. Fig. 1 (a, b) showed the aggregated nanowires-like morphology of bulk MnO2. Fig. 1(c) shows the EDS spectrum of the bulk MnO2, which confirms the formation as well as purity of MnO2. Further, the prepared CA-MnO2 QDs from bulk MnO2 was characterized by different analysis. The morphology, shape, and size of the prepared CA-MnO2 QDs were analyzed by using TEM analysis.

Fig. 2 (a, b) shows the TEM images of CA-MnO2 QDs in which particles are dispersed uniformly and nearly in a spherical shape. Fig. 2(c) shows the histogram distribution of particle size with a range of 2 to 5 nm and the average particle size was measured to be 3.4 nm. Fig. 2(d) displays the SAED ring pattern of CA-MnO2 QDs, which shows that they are crystalline in nature. The diameter of the diffraction rings present in the SAED pattern is related to (h2 + k2 + l2)2 in which (hkl) corresponds to Miller indices of the lattice planes, that are measured from the center of the ring. The major rings represent the (201), (401), (002), and (312) planes of MnO2 corresponding to the ICDD-JCPDS No. 72–1983. Fig. 2(e) shows the EDS spectra of CA-MnO2 QDs, which exhibit the presence of Mn, O, and C elements. The hydrodynamic particle size of CA-MnO2 QDs was measured by DLS data (Figure S1) and it was found to be ∼ 32 nm.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the XRD spectra of the prepared CA-MnO2 QDs, which exhibit the major peaks at 36.1°, 50.5°, and 65.4° corresponding to (201), (401), and (002) planes of MnO2 (ICDD-JCPDS No. 72–1983). A broad peak was also observed in the XRD pattern due to the overlapping of the 19.0° and 22.0° which indexed to the (200) and (110) planes of MnO2, respectively. Further, the composition and chemical state of CA-MnO2 QDs was confirmed by the XPS analysis. The survey spectrum of CA-MnO2 QDs shows prominent peaks which validate the presence of C, O, and Mn. Fig. 3(c) shows two major peaks at 639.5 eV and 651.4 eV, corresponding to Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 and their energy gap is 11.7 eV which confirmed the formation of MnO2. Fig. 3(d) shows the peak at 284.2 eV attributed to the O1s.

CA-MnO2 QDs were prepared from bulk MnO2 nanomaterial in the presence of citric acid by continuous ultrasonication of 10 h. Similarly, MnO2 QDs were also prepared in the presence of other reductive biomolecules such as cysteine, glutathione, and ascorbic acid. The influence of citric acid and other biomolecules on the photophysical properties of MnO2 QDs was analysed by UV–visible spectrum and PL emission spectra. Fig. 4 (a) shows the bar graph of MnO2 QDs functionalized with different biomolecules. Among all different functionalized MnO2 QDs, the CA-MnO2 QDs showed a high PL intensity (Fig. S2(a)). This indicates that citric acid provides a more stabilizing environment in the enhancement of fluorescence of MnO2 QDs as compared to the other used biomolecules. Citric acid act as reducing agent, size controller, and steric stabilizer for the synthesis of metal nanoparticles [25]. Citric acid contains free three carboxylic groups as compared to other biomolecules which lead to enhance the aqueous solubility and stability of MnO2 QDs. Generally, MnO2 nanostructure have less solubility in aqueous medium. However, the surface modification of MnO2 QDs with citric acid provides many free carboxylic groups on its surface that leads to enhance its water solubility and stability. Surface modification of CA-MnO2 QDs enhance the surface defects that result in increase of its fluorescence. Citric acid also acts as chelating agent [26] due to which CA-MnO2 QDs can be further used for selective metal ions detection. Fig. 4(b) displays the UV–vis absorbance, excitation, and emission spectra of CA-MnO2 QDs, which consist of peaks near about 245 nm, 300 nm, and 420 nm, respectively. Fig. 4(c) shows the PL emission spectra of CA-MnO2 QDs when excited at different wavelengths (280–380 nm). The maximum PL emission intensity of CA-MnO2 QDs was observed with an excitation wavelength of 300 nm. The PL emission peak gets red-shifted from 418 nm to 460 nm, with the variation in the excitation wavelength ranging from 280 to 380 nm. Therefore, CA-MnO2 QDs showed an excitation-dependent PL emission behaviour which may be due to the quantum confinement effect and surface defects of CA-MnO2 QDs [27]. The PL quantum yield of the prepared CA-MnO2 QDs was evaluated to be 13.3 % using equation (1), in which quinine sulphate was used as a reference solution.

Further, the PL emission of CA-MnO2 QDs was observed in the different concentrations of citric acid (Fig. S2(b)). The PL emission intensity was maximum observed when an equal ratio of MnO2 and citric acid was added for the preparation of CA-MnO2 QDs. The PL stability of CA-MnO2 QDs was also investigated by recording the PL intensity at different irradiation times (Fig. 4(d)). This shows that the prepared CA-MnO2 QDs have high photostability. The PL intensity of prepared CA-MnO2 QDs was measured at different pH range from 2 to 12 (Figure S3). It was found that the PL intensity gets increased from the pH value of 2 to 4, further, it becomes constant up to 9 pH value, and then it gets decreased from 10 to 12. The increase or decrease in the pH values was because of protonation or deprotonation of different emissive traps of CA-MnO2 QDs, which leads to a change in the PL intensity. Therefore, the pH ranges from 5 to 9 correspond to the buffer zone of the prepared sensing system, which was used for further application. The prepared CA-MnO2 QDs have shown good photophysical properties, due to which they can be further used for sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of Fe3+ ions by CA-MnO2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The PL intensity of CA-MnO2 QDs was measured in the presence of different metal ions (Fe2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Li+) with a specific concentration (Figure S4(a)). The PL intensity of the prepared fluorescent probe gets effectively quenched in the case of Fe3+ ions.

Further, the relative percentage change in the PL intensity of CA-MnO2 QDs with the addition of different metal ions was also estimated (Fig. 5 (a)). It was observed that the CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ system has 4.7 % PL intensity w.r.t. CA-MnO2 QDs (100 %). This may be due to the formation of a coordination complex between CA-MnO2 QDs and Fe3+ ions. The selectivity of the proposed system for Fe3+ ions was also investigated under the UV light of 365 nm wavelength (Fig. 5(b)). It was found that the prepared system has high selectivity for Fe3+ ions as compared to the other ions. For more selectivity, the interference study of prepared system was also performed by mixing Fe3+ ions with other metal ions in ratio of 1:3 (Fig. 6 (a)). There is no significant interference effect of other metal ions with Fe3+ ions were found.

To demonstrate the sensitivity of prepared CA-MnO2 QDs for Fe3+ ions the different concentrations (0.1 µM to 10 µM) of Fe3+ ions were added to the prepared fluorescent probe. Fig. 6(b) represents that the PL intensity of CA-MnO2 QDs was reduced by increasing the concentration of Fe3+ ions in the sensing system. The Stern-Volmer graph was plotted to examine and comprehend the PL quenching behavior of the prepared fluorescent sensing probe (CA-MnO2 QDs) with the addition of Fe3+ ions as a quencher. Fig. 6(c) shows a linear Stern–Volmer plot between the PL responses (F0/F) and different concentrations of Fe3+ ions on the basis of the following relation.
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]

Where F0 and F are PL intensities of CA-MnO2 QDs without and with the addition of Fe3+ ions, [Q] denotes the concentration of Fe3+ ions (quencher), and KSV represents the Stern–Volmer quenching constant. The Stern-Volmer constant was calculated to be 4.2 × 106 M−1 with the R2 value of 0.983 from Fig. 6(c). Fig. 6(d) shows a linear plot between PL responses (F0-F/F0) and various concentrations of Fe3+ ions (0 µM to 10 µM) with the R2 value of 0.989. The inset image of Fig. 6(d) shows a linear relationship between the PL responses (F0-F/F0) and different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (0 µM to 0.6 µM) with the value of R2 i.e., 0.997. From the above plot, the limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ was calculated to be 43 nM. The prepared CA-MnO2 QDs show an outstanding LOD value in comparison with other sensing platforms (Table 1 ).

Ascorbic acid act as a brilliant natural antioxidant and a good reducing agent for the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ [38]. We observed from the above studies that the PL intensity of CA-MnO2 QDs gets quenched highly in the case of Fe3+ as compared to Fe2+. Therefore, CA-MnO2 QDs can become an efficient sensing platform for the detection of AA based on the “turn-off–on” approach. The quenched PL intensity of the CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ sensing system was recovered by the addition of AA. Fig. S4(b) shows the PL intensity of the CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ sensing system in the presence of different analytes such as AA, glutathione (GSH), diphenylcarbzide (DPC), Dopamine (DA), Potassium hydrogen phosphate (monobasic) (M-PO4), Potassium dihydrogen phosphate (dibasic) (M-PO4), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), sodium fluoride (NaF), glucose, cysteine (Cys), methionine (Met), melamine (Mel), glutamic acid (Glu), thiourea, alanine (Ala), phenylalanine (Phe), tyrosine (Tyr), Glutamine (Gln), tryptophan (Trp), Catechol, leucine (Leu), histidine (His), and glycine (Gly). Fig. 7 (a) exhibits a bar graph in which the PL intensity of CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ gets highly recovered in the presence of AA as compared to the other analytes. Fig. 7(b) shows the selectivity image of CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ in the presence of different analytes under the UV light with 365 nm wavelength. It was found that the CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ sensing system shows high selectivity for AA as compare to other analytes. The interference study of CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ sensing system for AA was determined by mixing AA and other analytes in ratio of 1:3 (Fig. 8 (a)). It was found that the prepared system has high selectivity for the AA as compare to other analytes.

The fluorescence restoration of CA-MnO2 QDs was obtained by the addition of different concentrations of AA in the CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ sensing system (Fig. 8(b)). This shows that the increase in the concentration of AA in the CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ sensing system results in gradual fluorescence enhancement (“turn-on”) of CA-MnO2 QDs. The PL intensity was 80 % recovered with the addition of 25 µM of AA and further, no change was observed in PL intensity of CA-MnO2 QDs by increasing the concentration of AA. Fig. 8(c) shows a linear fit plot of PL responses (F-F0/F0) with concentration of AA (0–25 µM) with a linear equation F-F0/F0 = 0.124[AA] + 0.828 (R2 = 0.989). The inset image of Fig. 8(c) illustrates a linear relationship between PL responses (F-F0/F0) and at lower concentrations of AA (0–1.0 µM) with the linear equation F-F0/F0 = 1.126[AA] + 0.016 (R2 = 0.996). It shows an excellent LOD value, i.e., 90 nM for AA. In comparison with various sensing probes, the prepared sensing system showed the best LOD value (Table 2 ).

The PL quenching mechanism for a fluorescent molecule in the presence of hindering molecules is mostly static or dynamic quenching [14]. The high PL intensity of CA-MnO2 QDs gets quenched in the presence of Fe3+ ions (quencher). The static quenching occurs when the interaction between fluorescent molecule and quencher results in the formation of a non-fluorescent complex in the ground state [27]. While, the dynamic quenching occurs due to the collision of fluorescent molecule and quencher in the excited state, and then complex come back to the ground state by charge transfer [49]. In the case of static quenching, the ratio of fluorescence lifetime of the excited state to the ground state is equal to unity (τ0/τ = 1). In dynamic quenching the fluorescence lifetime changes in the presence and absence of a quencher. The time-resolved lifetime measurements of CA-MnO2 QDs without and with the addition of Fe3+ ions showed variation in the average lifetime (Fig. 8(d)) (Table S1). The average lifetime of CA-MnO2 QDs was found to be 6.29 ns, which demonstrates a very fast exciton recombination process. Further, the average lifetime of CA-MnO2 QDs was found to be 1.73 ns after the addition of Fe3+ ions. This indicates that the fluorescence quenching mechanism between the CA-MnO2 QDs and Fe3+ ions is dynamic quenching. The dynamic quenching mechanism can be attributed to the charge transfer process in the excited state, which occurs from the surface of CA-MnO2 QDs to the half-filled 3d orbital of Fe3+ ions. The prepared CA-MnO2 QDs contain oxygen-functionalized groups on their surface, which were confirmed by XPS analysis. This leads to the binding of Fe3+ ions on the surface of CA-MnO2 QDs, which results in non-radiative electron-hole recombination annihilation and further, it shows the fluorescence quenching [31]. Fe3+ ions exhibited a great chelating speed for the development of a chelation or coordination complex in comparison with the other metal ions because of large thermodynamic affinity [50]. The binding features of CA-MnO2 QDs with Fe3+ ions were also confirmed by the TEM analysis. Figure S5 shows that the well-dispersed particles of CA-MnO2 QDs were get aggregated in the presence of Fe3+ ions because they bind with the oxygen-functionalized groups present on the surface of CA-MnO2 QDs. This results in the decrease of fluorescence intensity of CA-MnO2 QDs. The TEM analysis was further done after the addition of AA in the CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ system as shown in Figure S6 and it was found that the aggregation of particles gets decreased. This confirms that Fe3+ ions get released from the surface of CA-MnO2 QDs due to redox reaction between AA and Fe3+ ions which further result in fluorescence restoration of CA-MnO2 QDs.

Further, the fluorescence quantum yield of CA-MnO2 QDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions and AA was evaluated by using equation (1) (Table 3 ). The addition of Fe3+ ions in CA-MnO2 QDs results as decrease in fluorescence quantum yield from 13.3 % to 2.0 %. Further, the addition of AA in CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ system leads to restore the fluorescence quantum yield i.e., 9 %. Further, the values of fluorescence quantum yield and average lifetime were used to determine different photophysical parameters. Moreover, to demonstrate the fluorescence quenching mechanism, we have calculated the radiative (kr ) and non-radiative (knr ) rate constants through the following equation [49]:
(3)
k r
=
ϕ f
τ f
(4)
1
τ f
=
k r
+
k
nr

where, τav corresponds to average lifetime and Φf corresponds to fluorescence quantum yield. The values of kr and knr values are mentioned in Table 3. The value of kr gets decreased, whereas the value of knr gets increased with the addition of Fe3+ ions in the CA-MnO2 QDs. This results suggest that the fluorescence quenching mechanism is based on the excited-state electron transfer [3]. The rate of electron transfer (kET ) was estimated to find the excited-state dynamics of the prepared sensing system via the following equation:
(5)
k
ET
=
1
τ
DA
-
1
τ D

where “τD ” and “τDA” indicate the average lifetime values of CA-MnO2 QDs without and with the addition of Fe3+ ions. The electron transfer efficiency (ΦEET ) is determined through the following equation:
(6)
Φ
EET
= 1 -
ϕ
DA
ϕ D
= 1 -
τ
DA
τ D

where, “ΦD ” and “ΦDA ” designate the quantum yields of CA-MnO2 QDs without and with the existence of Fe3+ ions. The effective values of kET and ΦEET leads to the transfer of electron in the excited state that results in dynamic quenching among the CA-MnO2 QDs and Fe3+ ions (Table 3). Further, to confirm the dynamic quenching the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) studies of CA-MnO2 QDs in the presence different concentrations of Fe3+ ions were performed (Figure S7). There is significant change in the TRPL spectra of CA-MnO2 QDs was observed after the addition of different concentrations (1 µM to 50 µM) of Fe3+ ions and this confirms the dynamic quenching. The average lifetime values of CA-MnO2 QDs in presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions are mentioned in Table S1.

The addition of AA in the CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ sensing system leads to the fluorescence recovery of CA-MnO2 QDs, which was also confirmed by the fluorescence quantum yield and average lifetime values (Table 3). The increase in the value of kr and decrease in the value of knr shows the removal of Fe3+ ions from the surface of the CA-MnO2 QDs. Furthermore, the variation in the values of kET and ΦEET with the addition of AA in CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ demonstrates the interruption of the energy-transfer process. This was due to a redox reaction between the AA and Fe3+ ions, which leads to the reduction of Fe3+ ions to Fe2+ ions by AA [19].

The practicability of our prepared CA-MnO2 QDs for the detection of Fe3+ ions and AA in a real sample was analyzed. The iron supplement (Fericit (ferric citrate), Septalyst) and vitamin C supplement (Limcee Vitamin C 500 mg) was bought from the local market. The above iron supplement contains 210 mg of iron and the vitamin C supplement contains 500 mg of ascorbic acid. The values of recovery rate (%), recovery error (%), relative standard deviation (RSD) are summarized in Table 4 , which shows that the experiment has given satisfactory results for practical applications. The mass plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (MP-AES) and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) have been done to validate the accuracy of our results for Fe3+ and AA detection, respectively. HPLC analysis of AA dissolved in aqueous medium at different concentration was performed. Fig. S8(a) represents the HPLC chromatogram of AA prepared in aqueous solution with different concentrations and Fig. S8(b) shows a linear plot of the integrated peak area in HPLC vs the different concentration of AA. From the calibration curve Fig. S8(b), we have validated the real sample (vitamin C supplement) analysis of AA. These results demonstrated that this PL “turn-off–on” sensor based on CA-MnO2 QDs is applicable for real sample analysis.

In summary, we have successfully developed citric acid-functionalized MnO2 quantum dots (CA-MnO2 QDs) by using bulk MnO2 as a precursor and citric acid as an exfoliating agent. The morphology and size of the prepared CA-MnO2 QDs were confirmed by TEM images and their surface composition was determined by XPS analysis. The synthesized CA-MnO2 QDs show high PL intensity and excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior due to variation in the particle size and surface defects. CA-MnO2 QDs exhibited good fluorescence quantum yield, high photostability, good pH stability within the range of 5–9, and high aqueous solubility. CA-MnO2 QDs were used as a “turn-off” sensor for the detection of Fe3+ ions with a limit of detection of 43 nM. The decrease in the value of fluorescence quantum yield and the average lifetime of CA-MnO2 QDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions confirmed the dynamic quenching mechanism between the CA-MnO2 QDs and Fe3+ ions. Further, we have also calculated the different photophysical parameters which explain the proposed fluorescence quenching mechanism in detail. The PL intensity of CA-MnO2 QDs was restored (“turn-on”) with the addition of AA in the prepared CA-MnO2 QDs-Fe3+ sensing probe based on the redox reaction. Among various sensing probes, the proposed sensing system is fast, cheap, environmentally friendly and also shows the good detection limit for Fe3+ ions (43 nM) and AA (90 nM). The prepared sensing probe was also used in real sample analysis of Fe3+ ions and AA which was further validated by MP-AES and HPLC, respectively.

Neeraj Sohal: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Banibrata Maity: Supervision, Conceptualization, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Soumen Basu: Supervision, Conceptualization, Visualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Neeraj sohal is grateful to CSIR (Grant no: 01 (2955)/18/EMR-II) for fellowship. Dr. Banibrata Maity is also thankful to Seed Grant Project, TIET, Patiala for research funding. Also, the authors are thankful for the TEM facility, funded by a TPF Nanomission, GOI project at the Centre for Nano and Soft Matter Sciences, Bengaluru, FESEM facility to TIET, Patiala. The authors are grateful to Dr. Nimai Mishra, SRM University, Amaravati, for lifetime analysis. The authors are also thankful to Reviewers for their careful revision and valuable comments which improved our manuscript quality.
